era_context: Mid-1st century CE. Roman Empire under Claudius is stable and expanding. Britain has been conquered (43 CE) and is being consolidated. Judea is under Roman procuratorial rule with growing Jewish resistance. Early Christianity is spreading through the Mediterranean. The Eastern Han Dynasty in China is well-established. The Silk Road trade networks are developing.

events.title: Paul's first missionary journey concludes; return to Antioch
events.description: The Apostle Paul, accompanied by Barnabas and John Mark, completes his first missionary journey through Cyprus and Asia Minor (Galatia), establishing churches and converting both Jews and Gentiles. He returns to Antioch, where he reports to the church leadership. This journey marks the beginning of Paul's major role in spreading Christianity beyond Jerusalem and establishes the pattern of his missionary work.
events.certainty_note: Acts provides a narrative of Paul's first journey, but precise dating is uncertain. Scholars typically date this journey to 47–48 CE or 48–49 CE. The year 48 CE is conventional for the conclusion.

events.title: Roman military campaigns in Britain consolidate conquest
events.description: Following the invasion of 43 CE, Roman legions under Aulus Plautius and his successors continue consolidating control of southern Britain. Fortifications are established, and resistance from British tribes is suppressed. The conquest is gradual and faces ongoing resistance, but by 48 CE, Roman control of the southeast is secure.
key_figures: Aulus Plautius (Roman general, governor of Britain 43–47 CE)
Ostorius Scapula (Roman governor of Britain, 47–52 CE)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Roman forts and settlements

events.certainty_note: Roman conquest of Britain is well-documented in classical sources and confirmed by archaeology. The timeline of consolidation is secure.

events.title: Herod Agrippa II becomes king of Chalcis
events.description: Herod Agrippa II, grandson of Herod the Great, is appointed by Claudius as king of Chalcis (a small kingdom in the Anti-Lebanon region). This marks the beginning of his long reign and his role as a Roman-backed Jewish ruler. Agrippa II will later play a significant role in the Jewish-Roman conflicts of the 60s CE.

sources.name: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews, Book 20

events.certainty_note: Josephus provides detailed information about Herod Agrippa II's reign. The date of his appointment to Chalcis is traditionally placed at 48 CE, though some sources suggest 50 CE.

events.title: Han Dynasty military campaigns against the Xiongnu
events.description: The Eastern Han Dynasty continues military campaigns against the Xiongnu nomads in Central Asia, seeking to secure the Silk Road trade routes and expand Chinese influence. These campaigns are part of a broader strategy to control the western frontier and maintain stability.

sources.name: History of the Later Han (Hou Hanshu)

events.certainty_note: Han Dynasty military campaigns are documented in Chinese sources, but specific events for 48 CE are not precisely dated. This entry reflects the broader pattern of Han military activity during this period.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Paul's first missionary journey is debated. Some scholars place it earlier (46–47 CE) or later (49–50 CE). Acts provides a narrative framework but lacks precise chronological markers. The appointment of Herod Agrippa II to Chalcis is traditionally dated to 48 CE, but Josephus's account is written 100+ years later and may be imprecise. No major disconfirming evidence undermines the events listed, but their exact dating within 48 CE is approximate.
historiographic_note: Sources for 48 CE are moderate in quantity but often secondary. Roman sources (Tacitus, Cassius Dio) are written 50–150 years after the events. Early Christian sources (Acts) are primary but theologically motivated. Jewish sources (Josephus) are written c. 93–94 CE, nearly 50 years after the events. Chinese sources are more contemporary (compiled within centuries). Archaeological evidence from Britain provides material confirmation of Roman military activity. The record is weighted toward the Mediterranean world.

graph_edges.note: Paul's first missionary journey and the establishment of churches led to disputes over Jewish law and the Council of Jerusalem
Roman consolidation in Britain and Han military campaigns in Central Asia are parallel imperial expansion efforts
